note_data.title: Nitrogen and sulfur
keyPoints: Transition elements are found in the d-block and form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals
Scandium and zinc are not transition elements because Sc³⁺ has an empty d subshell and Zn²⁺ has a full d subshell
The 4s orbital is filled before the 3d orbital, but 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons when forming ions
Transition elements show variable oxidation states due to similar energy levels of 3d and 4s orbitals
Chromium has unusual electron configuration [Ar]3d⁵4s¹ and copper has [Ar]3d¹⁰4s¹
Common ions include Ti²⁺, V²⁺/³⁺/⁴⁺/⁵⁺, Cr²⁺/³⁺/⁶⁺, Mn²⁺/³⁺/⁴⁺/⁶⁺/⁷⁺, Fe²⁺/³⁺, Co²⁺/³⁺, Ni⁺/²⁺, Cu⁺/²⁺

definitions.definition: Elements found in the d-block of the periodic table that form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals
A subshell that can hold up to 10 electrons, with a characteristic shape

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of steps or detailed account of the process
Apply knowledge to unfamiliar situation - multiple valid answers possible

modelAnswer.question: Explain how sulfur dioxide contributes to environmental problems and describe one method used to reduce these problems. [6]
modelAnswer.answer: Sulfur dioxide dissolves in atmospheric water to form sulfurous acid (H₂SO₃). This can be oxidized to form sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄), creating acid rain with pH below 5.6. Acid rain damages limestone buildings by reacting with calcium carbonate to form calcium sulfate. It also acidifies lakes and soil, harming aquatic life and vegetation. One method to reduce these problems is flue gas desulfurization in power stations. Calcium carbonate or calcium oxide is used to react with sulfur dioxide: CaCO₃ + SO₂ → CaSO₃ + CO₂. This removes SO₂ from emissions before they enter the atmosphere.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: SO₂ dissolves to form sulfurous/sulfuric acid
Mark 2: Forms acid rain with low pH

commonMistakes: Thinking scandium and zinc are transition elements - they are d-block elements but not transition elements
Forgetting that 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons when forming ions, despite 4s being filled first
Not recognizing the unusual electron configurations of chromium [Ar]3d⁵4s¹ and copper [Ar]3d¹⁰4s¹
examinerTips: Remember the definition requires incomplete d orbitals in the ions, not just being in the d-block
Always remove 4s electrons first when writing ion electron configurations, even though they fill after 3d
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Industrial process flow diagram showing Haber process with recycling loop for unreacted gases, and Contact process showing the two-stage sulfur trioxide formation and absorption in concentrated sulfuric acid
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to describe the industrial production of ammonia and explain why high pressure is used in the Haber process
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing knowledge of the catalyst used in the Contact process for sulfuric acid production
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q6 (8 marks) — students had to write equations for nitrogen dioxide formation and explain acid rain effects
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — question required explanation of why sulfur dioxide is converted to sulfur trioxide before making sulfuric acid

workedExample.question: Ammonia is manufactured by the Haber process. State the raw materials, conditions and catalyst used. Explain why a compromise temperature is chosen. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify raw materials - nitrogen (from air) and hydrogen (from natural gas/steam)
Step 2: State conditions - high pressure (200-300 atm), moderate temperature (450°C), iron catalyst
Step 3: Explain temperature compromise - higher temperature increases reaction rate but decreases yield (exothermic reaction)
Step 4: Connect to Le Chatelier's principle - forward reaction is exothermic, so lower temperature favors product formation but slows reaction
workedExample.answer: Raw materials: nitrogen from air, hydrogen from natural gas. Conditions: high pressure (200-300 atm), temperature 450°C, iron catalyst. A compromise temperature is used because higher temperature increases reaction rate but decreases yield since the forward reaction is exothermic. 450°C gives reasonable rate and yield.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for nitrogen and hydrogen sources, 1 mark for pressure, 1 mark for temperature, 1 mark for catalyst, 1 mark for compromise explanation linking rate and yield

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the Contact process with the Haber process' — always check which industrial process the question is asking about before writing your answer

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often forgot to mention the catalyst when describing industrial processes' — always include the catalyst (iron for Haber, vanadium(V) oxide for Contact) in process descriptions
Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak responses failed to link environmental effects to the chemical properties' — when discussing acid rain, always connect SO₂/NO₂ dissolving in water to form acids
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw the nitrogen cycle showing: atmosphere containing N₂, soil with nitrates/nitrites, plants uptaking nitrogen compounds through roots, decomposing matter releasing ammonia, nitrifying bacteria converting NH₃→NO₂⁻→NO₃⁻, denitrifying bacteria converting nitrates back to N₂. Include arrows showing the cyclical flow and label all key compounds (N₂, NH₃, NO₂⁻, NO₃⁻) and processes (nitrogen fixation, nitrification, denitrification).
note_data.quickRecap: Transition elements are d-block elements that form stable ions with incomplete d orbitals. Scandium and zinc are exceptions as their ions have empty or full d subshells respectively. When forming ions, 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons, and transition elements typically show multiple oxidation states.
